content.thinking:
Now I understand the settings.json structure. I need to add a `Notification` hook (and possibly `Stop` hook) that plays a sound.

Looking at the Claude Code docs/hooks, the relevant event for "waiting for user approval" is:
- `Notification` - Claude Code notification events (fires when Claude wants to alert the user, e.g., waiting for input after long tasks)
- `Stop` - fires when the agent finishes responding

Actually, I should think carefully about which event maps to "needs your approval":

1. When Claude asks a question and waits: this is when Claude's turn ends (Stop event)
2. When a tool is blocked and needs approval: this would be at the tool approval prompt level, not a hook

The `Stop` hook fires every time Claude finishes a response and waits for input, which could be too frequent. The `Notification` hook seems better suited for alerting me when something important needs attention, like at the end of a long task. But for the specific case where Claude is asking for approval—that's essentially when Claude stops and waits for my response, so I might need to check if the message contains an approval request.

Actually, I think the practical solution is to use the `Notification` hook since Claude Code sends notifications when tasks complete, when waiting for input, or when something needs attention. I can set it up to play a sound using PowerShell's built-in beep function with a two-tone alert. Creating a two-tone beep notification using Console.Beep with different frequencies. For the settings.json structure, the Notification event hooks don't need a matcher field since they trigger when Claude is waiting for input, and I'm considering whether a Stop hook would be redundant or if Notification is the right event to use.

Now I need to create both the skill documentation file and configure the hook in settings.json, deciding on a pleasant sound that isn't jarring. I'm thinking about whether to include a test function so users can preview the sound before enabling it. Writing notification hook config...

input.content:
---
description: Plays a notification sound when Claude finishes a response and is waiting for your input or approval. Invoke this skill to test the sound, enable it, or disable it.
---

# Approval Sound

Plays a two-tone chime whenever Claude finishes responding and is waiting for your input — so you don't have to watch the screen.

The sound is configured as a `Notification` hook in `~/.claude/settings.json`.

## How to use this skill

When invoked, do one of these based on what the user asked:

### Test the sound
Run this PowerShell command to verify the sound works:
```powershell
[Console]::Beep(880, 200); Start-Sleep -Milliseconds 100; [Console]::Beep(1100, 300)
```
Tell the user: "Sound played — did you hear it?"

### Check if enabled
Read `~/.claude/settings.json` and check whether a `Notification` hook exists with a `[Console]::Beep` command. Report yes/no.

### Disable the sound
Remove the `Notification` hook block from `~/.claude/settings.json` that contains the Beep command. Confirm removal.

### Re-enable the sound
Add back the Notification hook (same command as configured originally).

## Current configuration

The hook is registered under `hooks.Notification` in `~/.claude/settings.json`:
- Trigger: every time Claude finishes and is waiting for input
- Sound: two ascending tones (880 Hz → 1100 Hz)
- Shell: PowerShell, no external dependencies

description: Plays a notification sound when Claude finishes a response and is waiting for your input or approval. Invoke this skill to test the sound, enable it, or disable it.

# Approval Sound

Plays a two-tone chime whenever Claude finishes responding and is waiting for your input — so you don't have to watch the screen.

The sound is configured as a `Notification` hook in `~/.claude/settings.json`.

## How to use this skill

When invoked, do one of these based on what the user asked:

### Test the sound
Run this PowerShell command to verify the sound works:
```powershell
[Console]::Beep(880, 200); Start-Sleep -Milliseconds 100; [Console]::Beep(1100, 300)
```
Tell the user: "Sound played — did you hear it?"

### Check if enabled
Read `~/.claude/settings.json` and check whether a `Notification` hook exists with a `[Console]::Beep` command. Report yes/no.

### Disable the sound
Remove the `Notification` hook block from `~/.claude/settings.json` that contains the Beep command. Confirm removal.

### Re-enable the sound
Add back the Notification hook (same command as configured originally).

## Current configuration

The hook is registered under `hooks.Notification` in `~/.claude/settings.json`:
- Trigger: every time Claude finishes and is waiting for input
- Sound: two ascending tones (880 Hz → 1100 Hz)
- Shell: PowerShell, no external dependencies

## claude-code-templates npm Package

The claude-code-templates package allows you to install specific components including agents like `development-tools/code-reviewer`. It's a comprehensive collection of AI agents, custom commands, settings, hooks, external integrations (MCPs), and project templates to enhance your development workflow.

## Code-Reviewer Skill

You can install the code-reviewer agent using the command: `npx claude-code-templates@latest --agent development-tools/code-reviewer --yes`.

There are also community code review skills available. Code Review Skill is a production-ready skill for Claude Code that transforms AI-assisted code review from vague suggestions into a structured, consistent, and expert-level process. It covers 20+ languages and frameworks with over 16,000 lines of carefully curated review guidelines — loaded progressively to minimize context window usage.

The search results show that code review capabilities are well-established in the Claude Code ecosystem as of 2026, with both official templates and third-party community skills available for developers to use.

## Primary Repository

The main repository is located at GitHub as davila7/claude-code-templates, which is a CLI tool for configuring and monitoring Claude Code.

## Package Details

It's a Node.js CLI tool for managing Claude Code components (agents, commands, MCPs, hooks, settings) with a static website for browsing and installing components. It provides a comprehensive collection of AI agents, custom commands, settings, hooks, external integrations (MCPs), and project templates to enhance development workflow.

## Installation

The package is available on npm with version 1.28.16 and has had 2,643 downloads. You can install it using:
- `npm install -g claude-code-templates@latest` (global installation)
- `npx claude-code-templates@latest` (without installation)

## Repository Links

- **GitHub**: https://github.com/davila7/claude-code-templates
- **NPM Package**: https://www.npmjs.com/package/claude-code-templates
- **Releases**: https://github.com/davila7/claude-code-templates/releases

The repository contains a comprehensive collection of components for enhancing Claude Code workflows, with over 100 agents, commands, settings, hooks, and MCPs available for installation.

content.content:
# Code Reviewer Skill Overview

## What It Is
The Code Reviewer is a Claude development skill providing "a complete toolkit for code reviewer with modern tools and best practices." It's available through the AI Templates platform as an installable skill.

## Core Functions
The skill offers three main capabilities:
1. **PR Analyzer** — Automates pull request analysis with scaffolding and quality checks
2. **Code Quality Checker** — Performs deep analysis with performance metrics and recommendations
3. **Review Report Generator** — Creates production-grade review documentation

## Dependencies & Requirements
The skill supports multiple languages (TypeScript, JavaScript, Python, Go, Swift, Kotlin) and various frameworks. It requires:
- Node.js/npm or Python with pip
- Standard development tools (Docker, Kubernetes optional)
- No mention of external APIs required

## Reference Materials
Three documentation files guide implementation:
- Code review checklist with patterns and anti-patterns
- Coding standards workflow documentation
- Common antipatterns technical reference

## Source & Author
Available on GitHub at `davila7/claude-code-templates`. Installation is via: `npx claude-code-templates@latest --skill development/code-reviewer`

The platform is supported by Vercel, Neon, and Claude's open-source programs.

content.content:
# Claude Code Templates Repository Structure

## Key Metrics
- **Stars:** 27.8k
- **Forks:** 2.9k
- **License:** MIT
- **Last Release:** v1.28.3 (November 15, 2025)
- **Open Issues:** 77

## Directory Structure

The repository contains these main directories:

- `.claude/` - Claude Code configuration
- `.github/` - GitHub workflows
- `.vscode/` - VS Code settings
- `api/` - Backend API
- `cli-tool/` - Command-line interface
- `cloudflare-workers/` - Cloudflare integrations
- `dashboard/` - Web UI
- `database/migrations/` - Database schemas
- `docs/` - Documentation
- `scripts/` - Utility scripts

## Skills Storage

Based on the repository structure and documentation, skills appear to be distributed across the codebase rather than in a single dedicated directory. The project aggregates skills from multiple sources:

- Official Anthropic skills
- Community contributions (obra, alirezarezvani, wshobson)
- K-Dense-AI scientific skills
- NerdyChefsAI enterprise skills

Skills are managed through the "Skills Manager" with "plugin skills support" as noted in the latest release, suggesting they're likely stored in configuration files or a skills registry system rather than in a traditional `skills/` directory.

content.content:
---
name: code-reviewer
description: Comprehensive code review skill for TypeScript, JavaScript, Python, Swift, Kotlin, Go. Includes automated code analysis, best practice checking, security scanning, and review checklist generation. Use when reviewing pull requests, providing code feedback, identifying issues, or ensuring code quality standards.
---

# Code Reviewer

Complete toolkit for code reviewer with modern tools and best practices.

## Quick Start

### Main Capabilities

This skill provides three core capabilities through automated scripts:

```bash
# Script 1: Pr Analyzer
python scripts/pr_analyzer.py [options]

# Script 2: Code Quality Checker
python scripts/code_quality_checker.py [options]

# Script 3: Review Report Generator
python scripts/review_report_generator.py [options]
```

## Core Capabilities

### 1. Pr Analyzer

Automated tool for pr analyzer tasks.

**Features:**
- Automated scaffolding
- Best practices built-in
- Configurable templates
- Quality checks

**Usage:**
```bash
python scripts/pr_analyzer.py <project-path> [options]
```

### 2. Code Quality Checker

Comprehensive analysis and optimization tool.

**Features:**
- Deep analysis
- Performance metrics
- Recommendations
- Automated fixes

**Usage:**
```bash
python scripts/code_quality_checker.py <target-path> [--verbose]
```

### 3. Review Report Generator

Advanced tooling for specialized tasks.

**Features:**
- Expert-level automation
- Custom configurations
- Integration ready
- Production-grade output

**Usage:**
```bash
python scripts/review_report_generator.py [arguments] [options]
```

## Reference Documentation

### Code Review Checklist

Comprehensive guide available in `references/code_review_checklist.md`:

- Detailed patterns and practices
- Code examples
- Best practices
- Anti-patterns to avoid
- Real-world scenarios

### Coding Standards

Complete workflow documentation in `references/coding_standards.md`:

- Step-by-step processes
- Optimization strategies
- Tool integrations
- Performance tuning
- Troubleshooting guide

### Common Antipatterns

Technical reference guide in `references/common_antipatterns.md`:

- Technology stack details
- Configuration examples
- Integration patterns
- Security considerations
- Scalability guidelines

## Tech Stack

**Languages:** TypeScript, JavaScript, Python, Go, Swift, Kotlin
**Frontend:** React, Next.js, React Native, Flutter
**Backend:** Node.js, Express, GraphQL, REST APIs
**Database:** PostgreSQL, Prisma, NeonDB, Supabase
**DevOps:** Docker, Kubernetes, Terraform, GitHub Actions, CircleCI
**Cloud:** AWS, GCP, Azure

## Development Workflow

### 1. Setup and Configuration

```bash
# Install dependencies
npm install
# or
pip install -r requirements.txt

# Configure environment
cp .env.example .env
```

### 2. Run Quality Checks

```bash
# Use the analyzer script
python scripts/code_quality_checker.py .

# Review recommendations
# Apply fixes
```

### 3. Implement Best Practices

Follow the patterns and practices documented in:
- `references/code_review_checklist.md`
- `references/coding_standards.md`
- `references/common_antipatterns.md`

## Best Practices Summary

### Code Quality
- Follow established patterns
- Write comprehensive tests
- Document decisions
- Review regularly

### Performance
- Measure before optimizing
- Use appropriate caching
- Optimize critical paths
- Monitor in production

### Security
- Validate all inputs
- Use parameterized queries
- Implement proper authentication
- Keep dependencies updated

### Maintainability
- Write clear code
- Use consistent naming
- Add helpful comments
- Keep it simple

## Common Commands

```bash
# Development
npm run dev
npm run build
npm run test
npm run lint

# Analysis
python scripts/code_quality_checker.py .
python scripts/review_report_generator.py --analyze

# Deployment
docker build -t app:latest .
docker-compose up -d
kubectl apply -f k8s/
```

## Troubleshooting

### Common Issues

Check the comprehensive troubleshooting section in `references/common_antipatterns.md`.

### Getting Help

- Review reference documentation
- Check script output messages
- Consult tech stack documentation
- Review error logs

## Resources

- Pattern Reference: `references/code_review_checklist.md`
- Workflow Guide: `references/coding_standards.md`
- Technical Guide: `references/common_antipatterns.md`
- Tool Scripts: `scripts/` directory

---
description: Plays a notification sound when Claude finishes a response and is waiting for your input or approval. Invoke this skill to test the sound, enable it, or disable it.
---

# Approval Sound

Plays a two-tone chime whenever Claude finishes responding and is waiting for your input — so you don't have to watch the screen.

The sound is configured as a `Notification` hook in `~/.claude/settings.json`.

## How to use this skill

When invoked, do one of these based on what the user asked:

### Test the sound
Run this PowerShell command to verify the sound works:
```powershell
[Console]::Beep(880, 200); Start-Sleep -Milliseconds 100; [Console]::Beep(1100, 300)
```
Tell the user: "Sound played — did you hear it?"

### Check if enabled
Read `~/.claude/settings.json` and check whether a `Notification` hook exists with a `[Console]::Beep` command. Report yes/no.

### Disable the sound
Remove the `Notification` hook block from `~/.claude/settings.json` that contains the Beep command. Confirm removal.

### Re-enable the sound
Add back the Notification hook (same command as configured originally).

## Current configuration

The hook is registered under `hooks.Notification` in `~/.claude/settings.json`:
- Trigger: every time Claude finishes and is waiting for input
- Sound: two ascending tones (880 Hz → 1100 Hz)
- Shell: PowerShell, no external dependencies

## Remotion Agent Skills for Claude Code

Remotion ships an official Agent Skills bundle so coding agents such as Claude Code learn Remotion timing, media, and composition rules—installable with one CLI line alongside new `bun create video` scaffolding.

Remotion maintains a list of Agent Skills that define best practices for working in Remotion projects. The repository is available on GitHub as `remotion-dev/skills` (not `remotion-dev/agent-skills`).

### What These Skills Do

The skills encode best practices for Remotion's React-based, programmatic video model so agents avoid common timing, asset, and composition mistakes. The Remotion skills include official Remotion skills for video creation in React with 28 modular rules.

### Usage in Claude Code

The Remotion agent skill for Claude Code translates natural language into working Remotion components. The workflow involves describing what you want in a prompt, Claude generates the React/Remotion code, you preview in the Remotion Studio, and render to MP4.

You can find the repository at `github.com/remotion-dev/skills` with detailed reference materials at `skills.sh/remotion-dev/skills/remotion-best-practices`.

The skills encode best practices for Remotion's React-based, programmatic video model so agents avoid common timing, asset, and composition mistakes. The Remotion skills include official Remotion skills for video creation in React with 28 modular rules.

### Usage in Claude Code

The Remotion agent skill for Claude Code translates natural language into working Remotion components. The workflow involves describing what you want in a prompt, Claude generates the React/Remotion code, you preview in the Remotion Studio, and render to MP4.

You can find the repository at `github.com/remotion-dev/skills` with detailed reference materials at `skills.sh/remotion-dev/skills/remotion-best-practices`.

file.content:
---
description: Plays a notification sound when Claude finishes a response and is waiting for your input or approval. Invoke this skill to test the sound, enable it, or disable it.
---

# Approval Sound

Plays a two-tone chime whenever Claude finishes responding and is waiting for your input — so you don't have to watch the screen.

The sound is configured as a `Notification` hook in `~/.claude/settings.json`.

## How to use this skill

When invoked, do one of these based on what the user asked:

### Test the sound
Run this command to verify the sound works:
```powershell
python -c "import winsound; winsound.PlaySound(r'C:\Windows\Media\tada.wav', winsound.SND_FILENAME | winsound.SND_SYNC)"
```
Tell the user: "Sound played — did you hear it?"

### Check if enabled
Read `~/.claude/settings.json` and check whether a `Notification` hook exists with a `[Console]::Beep` command. Report yes/no.

### Disable the sound
Remove the `Notification` hook block from `~/.claude/settings.json` that contains the Beep command. Confirm removal.

### Re-enable the sound
Add back the Notification hook (same command as configured originally).

## Current configuration

The hook is registered under `hooks.Notification` in `~/.claude/settings.json`:
- Trigger: every time Claude finishes and is waiting for input
- Sound: two ascending tones (880 Hz → 1100 Hz)
- Shell: PowerShell, no external dependencies

## claude.ai Microsoft Learn
# Microsoft Learn MCP Server

This server gives structured access to official Microsoft and Azure documentation via three tools:

## Tools

### microsoft_docs_search
Search official documentation and return up to 10 concise, high-quality content chunks (max 500 tokens each), including title, URL, and excerpt.

- Use first to get a quick, reliable overview
- Ideal for grounding answers in Microsoft knowledge

### microsoft_code_sample_search
Search for code snippets and examples in official Microsoft Learn documentation and return up to 20 relevant, high-quality code samples.

- Use when you need to provide sample Microsoft/Azure related code in your answers.  
- Ideal for generating code snippets or practical implementation examples.  
- Optional parameter `language` can filter results. 

### microsoft_docs_fetch
Fetch and convert full Microsoft documentation pages to markdown.

- Use after search when you need full content from a specific URL
- Required for detailed tutorials, troubleshooting, prerequisites, code samples, or when search results are incomplete or outdated

## Workflow

1. Use `microsoft_docs_search` to find relevant documents.
2. If you need code examples or practical snippets, use `microsoft_code_sample_search`.
3. If deeper or complete information is needed, use `microsoft_docs_fetch`.

**Search gives breadth. Code Sample Search gives practical examples. Fetch gives depth.**

content.content:
# Summary of gws — Google Workspace CLI

## Core Functionality
"One CLI for all of Google Workspace — built for humans and AI agents." The tool provides access to Drive, Gmail, Calendar, and all Workspace APIs through dynamically generated commands that reflect Google's Discovery Service in real-time.

## Installation Methods
- Pre-built binaries from GitHub Releases (recommended)
- npm: `npm install -g @googleworkspace/cli`
- Cargo: `cargo install --git https://github.com/googleworkspace/cli --locked`
- Homebrew: `brew install googleworkspace-cli`
- Nix flake support available

**Requirements:** Node.js 18+, Google Cloud project, Google Workspace account access.

## Authentication Approaches
1. **Interactive Desktop**: `gws auth setup` (requires gcloud) → `gws auth login`
   - Credentials encrypted with AES-256-GCM, stored in OS keyring
2. **Manual OAuth Setup**: Download client JSON to `~/.config/gws/client_secret.json`
3. **Environment Variables**: 
   - `GOOGLE_WORKSPACE_CLI_TOKEN` (access token)
   - `GOOGLE_WORKSPACE_CLI_CREDENTIALS_FILE` (OAuth or service account)
4. **Service Account**: Point to service account key file
5. **CI/Headless**: Export credentials from authenticated machine

**Priority order**: Access token → Credentials file → Encrypted credentials → Plaintext file

## Data Access
The CLI can access:
- Gmail messages and settings
- Google Drive files
- Google Calendar events
- Google Sheets
- Google Chat spaces
- Google Docs
- Google Tasks
- Any enabled Google Workspace API

Users control scope during authentication; testing-mode apps limited to ~25 scopes.

## Agent Skills
Over 100 included skills for common workflows (email, spreadsheet operations, file management, meeting preparation, standup reports). Skills installable via npm or symlinkable for OpenClaw integration.

## Key Features
- Structured JSON output throughout
- Auto-pagination support
- Dry-run previews
- Helper commands (prefixed with `+`) for common tasks
- Model Armor integration for response sanitization
- Gemini CLI extension support

## Exit Codes
- 0: Success
- 1: API error
- 2: Auth error
- 3: Validation error
- 4: Discovery error
- 5: Internal error

**Note:** This is an unofficial product, currently under active development toward v1.0.

# Security Issue Reporting

Based on the provided content, here's the key information:

**Where to Report:**
Use the dedicated vulnerability reporting portal at https://g.co/vulnz for all security issues.

**Process:**
The Google Security Team manages intake through that platform and coordinates disclosure via GitHub, including GitHub Security Advisory.

**Response Timeline:**
"The Google Security Team will respond within 5 working days of your report on https://g.co/vulnz."

---

**Note:** The provided excerpt covers only the security reporting process. The full security policy, data handling procedures, credential storage practices, and telemetry information you mentioned aren't included in the content shared, so I cannot address those topics.

# Google Workspace CLI Skills Directory

The skills directory contains 97 skill folders organized into three categories:

## Core Google Workspace Skills (gws-*)
These include integrations with Google services:
- **Admin & Reporting**: gws-admin-reports
- **Calendar**: gws-calendar, gws-calendar-agenda, gws-calendar-insert
- **Chat**: gws-chat, gws-chat-send
- **Classroom**: gws-classroom
- **Docs**: gws-docs, gws-docs-write
- **Drive**: gws-drive, gws-drive-upload
- **Events**: gws-events, gws-events-subscribe, gws-events-renew
- **Forms**: gws-forms
- **Gmail**: gws-gmail and 8 specialized modules (read, send, reply, forward, triage, watch, reply-all)
- **Keep**: gws-keep
- **Meet**: gws-meet
- **ModelArmor**: gws-modelarmor and 3 security modules
- **People**: gws-people
- **Sheets**: gws-sheets, gws-sheets-read, gws-sheets-append
- **Slides**: gws-slides
- **Tasks**: gws-tasks
- **Workflow**: gws-workflow and 5 workflow templates

## Persona Skills (persona-*)
Predefined role-based skill sets for 10 personas including content creators, event coordinators, executives, and project managers.

## Recipe Skills (recipe-*)
50+ pre-built automation templates for common workflows like email management, document creation, and scheduling.

content.content:
1	---
2	name: "google-workspace-cli"
3	description: "Google Workspace administration via the gws CLI. Install, authenticate, and automate Gmail, Drive, Sheets, Calendar, Docs, Chat, and Tasks. Run security audits, execute 43 built-in recipes, and use 10 persona bundles. Use for Google Workspace admin, gws CLI setup, Gmail automation, Drive management, or Calendar scheduling."
4	---
5	
6	# Google Workspace CLI
7	
8	Expert guidance and automation for Google Workspace administration using the open-source `gws` CLI. Covers installation, authentication, 18+ service APIs, 43 built-in recipes, and 10 persona bundles for role-based workflows.
9	
10	---
11	
12	## Quick Start
13	
14	### Check Installation
15	
16	```bash
17	# Verify gws is installed and authenticated
18	python3 scripts/gws_doctor.py
19	```
20	
21	### Send an Email
22	
23	```bash
24	gws gmail users.messages send me --to "team@company.com" \
25	  --subject "Weekly Update" --body "Here's this week's summary..."
26	```
27	
28	### List Drive Files
29	
30	```bash
31	gws drive files list --json --limit 20 | python3 scripts/output_analyzer.py --select "name,mimeType,modifiedTime" --format table
32	```
33	
34	---
35	
36	## Installation
37	
38	### npm (recommended)
39	
40	```bash
41	npm install -g @anthropic/gws
42	gws --version
43	```
44	
45	### Cargo (from source)
46	
47	```bash
48	cargo install gws-cli
49	gws --version
50	```
51	
52	### Pre-built Binaries
53	
54	Download from [github.com/googleworkspace/cli/releases](https://github.com/googleworkspace/cli/releases) for macOS, Linux, or Windows.
55	
56	### Verify Installation
57	
58	```bash
59	python3 scripts/gws_doctor.py
60	# Checks: PATH, version, auth status, service connectivity
61	```
62	
63	---
64	
65	## Authentication
66	
67	### OAuth Setup (Interactive)
68	
69	```bash
70	# Step 1: Create Google Cloud project and OAuth credentials
71	python3 scripts/auth_setup_guide.py --guide oauth
72	
73	# Step 2: Run auth setup
74	gws auth setup
75	
76	# Step 3: Validate
77	gws auth status --json
78	```
79	
80	### Service Account (Headless/CI)
81	
82	```bash
83	# Generate setup instructions
84	python3 scripts/auth_setup_guide.py --guide service-account
85	
86	# Configure with key file
87	export GWS_SERVICE_ACCOUNT_KEY=/path/to/key.json
88	export GWS_DELEGATED_USER=admin@company.com
89	gws auth status
90	```
91	
92	### Environment Variables
93	
94	```bash
95	# Generate .env template
96	python3 scripts/auth_setup_guide.py --generate-env
97	```
98	
99	| Variable | Purpose |
100	|----------|---------|
101	| `GWS_CLIENT_ID` | OAuth client ID |
102	| `GWS_CLIENT_SECRET` | OAuth client secret |
103	| `GWS_TOKEN_PATH` | Custom token storage path |
104	| `GWS_SERVICE_ACCOUNT_KEY` | Service account JSON key path |
105	| `GWS_DELEGATED_USER` | User to impersonate (service accounts) |
106	| `GWS_DEFAULT_FORMAT` | Default output format (json/ndjson/table) |
107	
108	### Validate Authentication
109	
110	```bash
111	python3 scripts/auth_setup_guide.py --validate --json
112	# Tests each service endpoint
113	```
114	
115	---
116	
117	## Workflow 1: Gmail Automation
118	
119	**Goal:** Automate email operations — send, search, label, and filter management.
120	
121	### Send and Reply
122	
123	```bash
124	# Send a new email
125	gws gmail users.messages send me --to "client@example.com" \
126	  --subject "Proposal" --body "Please find attached..." \
127	  --attachment proposal.pdf
128	
129	# Reply to a thread
130	gws gmail users.messages reply me --thread-id <THREAD_ID> \
131	  --body "Thanks for your feedback..."
132	
133	# Forward a message
134	gws gmail users.messages forward me --message-id <MSG_ID> \
135	  --to "manager@company.com"
136	```
137	
138	### Search and Filter
139	
140	```bash
141	# Search emails
142	gws gmail users.messages list me --query "from:client@example.com after:2025/01/01" --json \
143	  | python3 scripts/output_analyzer.py --count
144	
145	# List labels
146	gws gmail users.labels list me --json
147	
148	# Create a filter
149	gws gmail users.settings.filters create me \
150	  --criteria '{"from":"notifications@service.com"}' \
151	  --action '{"addLabelIds":["Label_123"],"removeLabelIds":["INBOX"]}'
152	```
153	
154	### Bulk Operations
155	
156	```bash
157	# Archive all read emails older than 30 days
158	gws gmail users.messages list me --query "is:read older_than:30d" --json \
159	  | python3 scripts/output_analyzer.py --select "id" --format json \
160	  | xargs -I {} gws gmail users.messages modify me {} --removeLabelIds INBOX
161	```
162	
163	---
164	
165	## Workflow 2: Drive & Sheets
166	
167	**Goal:** Manage files, create spreadsheets, configure sharing, and export data.
168	
169	### File Operations
170	
171	```bash
172	# List files
173	gws drive files list --json --limit 50 \
174	  | python3 scripts/output_analyzer.py --select "name,mimeType,size" --format table
175	
176	# Upload a file
177	gws drive files create --name "Q1 Report" --upload report.pdf \
178	  --parents <FOLDER_ID>
179	
180	# Create a Google Sheet
181	gws sheets spreadsheets create --title "Budget 2026" --json
182	
183	# Download/export
184	gws drive files export <FILE_ID> --mime "application/pdf" --output report.pdf
185	```
186	
187	### Sharing
188	
189	```bash
190	# Share with user
191	gws drive permissions create <FILE_ID> \
192	  --type user --role writer --emailAddress "colleague@company.com"
193	
194	# Share with domain (view only)
195	gws drive permissions create <FILE_ID> \
196	  --type domain --role reader --domain "company.com"
197	
198	# List who has access
199	gws drive permissions list <FILE_ID> --json
200	```
201	
202	### Sheets Data
203	
204	```bash
205	# Read a range
206	gws sheets spreadsheets.values get <SHEET_ID> --range "Sheet1!A1:D10" --json
207	
208	# Write data
209	gws sheets spreadsheets.values update <SHEET_ID> --range "Sheet1!A1" \
210	  --values '[["Name","Score"],["Alice",95],["Bob",87]]'
211	
212	# Append rows
213	gws sheets spreadsheets.values append <SHEET_ID> --range "Sheet1!A1" \
214	  --values '[["Charlie",92]]'
215	```
216	
217	---
218	
219	## Workflow 3: Calendar & Meetings
220	
221	**Goal:** Schedule events, find available times, and generate standup reports.
222	
223	### Event Management
224	
225	```bash
226	# Create an event
227	gws calendar events insert primary \
228	  --summary "Sprint Planning" \
229	  --start "2026-03-15T10:00:00" --end "2026-03-15T11:00:00" \
230	  --attendees "team@company.com" \
231	  --location "Conference Room A"
232	
233	# List upcoming events
234	gws calendar events list primary --timeMin "$(date -u +%Y-%m-%dT%H:%M:%SZ)" \
235	  --maxResults 10 --json
236	
237	# Quick event (natural language)
238	gws helpers quick-event "Lunch with Sarah tomorrow at noon"
239	```
240	
241	### Find Available Time
242	
243	```bash
244	# Check free/busy for multiple people
245	gws helpers find-time \
246	  --attendees "alice@co.com,bob@co.com,charlie@co.com" \
247	  --duration 60 --within "2026-03-15,2026-03-19" --json
248	```
249	
250	### Standup Report
251	
252	```bash
253	# Generate daily standup from calendar + tasks
254	gws recipes standup-report --json \
255	  | python3 scripts/output_analyzer.py --format table
256	
257	# Meeting prep (agenda + attendee info)
258	gws recipes meeting-prep --event-id <EVENT_ID>
259	```
260	
261	---
262	
263	## Workflow 4: Security Audit
264	
265	**Goal:** Audit Google Workspace security configuration and generate remediation commands.
266	
267	### Run Full Audit
268	
269	```bash
270	# Full audit across all services
271	python3 scripts/workspace_audit.py --json
272	
273	# Audit specific services
274	python3 scripts/workspace_audit.py --services gmail,drive,calendar
275	
276	# Demo mode (no gws required)
277	python3 scripts/workspace_audit.py --demo
278	```
279	
280	### Audit Checks
281	
282	| Area | Check | Risk |
283	|------|-------|------|
284	| Drive | External sharing enabled | Data exfiltration |
285	| Gmail | Auto-forwarding rules | Data exfiltration |
286	| Gmail | DMARC/SPF/DKIM records | Email spoofing |
287	| Calendar | Default sharing visibility | Information leak |
288	| OAuth | Third-party app grants | Unauthorized access |
289	| Admin | Super admin count | Privilege escalation |
290	| Admin | 2-Step verification enforcement | Account takeover |
291	
292	### Review and Remediate
293	
294	```bash
295	# Review findings
296	python3 scripts/workspace_audit.py --json | python3 scripts/output_analyzer.py \
297	  --filter "status=FAIL" --select "area,check,remediation"
298	
299	# Execute remediation (example: restrict external sharing)
300	gws drive about get --json  # Check current settings
301	# Follow remediation commands from audit output
302	```
303	
304	---
305	
306	## Python Tools
307	
308	| Script | Purpose | Usage |
309	|--------|---------|-------|
310	| `gws_doctor.py` | Pre-flight diagnostics | `python3 scripts/gws_doctor.py [--json] [--services gmail,drive]` |
311	| `auth_setup_guide.py` | Guided auth setup | `python3 scripts/auth_setup_guide.py --guide oauth` |
312	| `gws_recipe_runner.py` | Recipe catalog & runner | `python3 scripts/gws_recipe_runner.py --list [--persona pm]` |
313	| `workspace_audit.py` | Security/config audit | `python3 scripts/workspace_audit.py [--json] [--demo]` |
314	| `output_analyzer.py` | JSON/NDJSON analysis | `gws ... --json \| python3 scripts/output_analyzer.py --count` |
315	
316	All scripts are stdlib-only, support `--json` output, and include demo mode with embedded sample data.
317	
318	---
319	
320	## Best Practices
321	
322	### Security
323	
324	1. Use OAuth with minimal scopes — request only what each workflow needs
325	2. Store tokens in the system keyring, never in plain text files
326	3. Rotate service account keys every 90 days
327	4. Audit third-party OAuth app grants quarterly
328	5. Use `--dry-run` before bulk destructive operations
329	
330	### Automation
331	
332	1. Pipe `--json` output through `output_analyzer.py` for filtering and aggregation
333	2. Use recipes for multi-step operations instead of chaining raw commands
334	3. Select a persona bundle to scope recipes to your role
335	4. Use NDJSON format (`--format ndjson`) for streaming large result sets
336	5. Set `GWS_DEFAULT_FORMAT=json` in your shell profile for scripting
337	
338	### Performance
339	
340	1. Use `--fields` to request only needed fields (reduces payload size)
341	2. Use `--limit` to cap results when browsing
342	3. Use `--page-all` only when you need complete datasets
343	4. Batch operations with recipes rather than individual API calls
344	5. Cache frequently accessed data (e.g., label IDs, folder IDs) in variables
345	
346	---
347	
348	## Limitations
349	
350	| Constraint | Impact |
351	|------------|--------|
352	| OAuth tokens expire after 1 hour | Re-auth needed for long-running scripts |
353	| API rate limits (per-user, per-service) | Bulk operations may hit 429 errors |
354	| Scope requirements vary by service | Must request correct scopes during auth |
355	| Pre-v1.0 CLI status | Breaking changes possible between releases |
356	| Google Cloud project required | Free, but requires setup in Cloud Console |
357	| Admin API needs admin privileges | Some audit checks require Workspace Admin role |
358	
359	### Required Scopes by Service
360	
361	```bash
362	# List scopes for specific services
363	python3 scripts/auth_setup_guide.py --scopes gmail,drive,calendar,sheets
364	```
365	
366	| Service | Key Scopes |
367	|---------|-----------|
368	| Gmail | `gmail.modify`, `gmail.send`, `gmail.labels` |
369	| Drive | `drive.file`, `drive.metadata.readonly` |
370	| Sheets | `spreadsheets` |
371	| Calendar | `calendar`, `calendar.events` |
372	| Admin | `admin.directory.user.readonly`, `admin.directory.group` |
373	| Tasks | `tasks` |
374

file.content:
---
name: "google-workspace-cli"
description: "Google Workspace administration via the gws CLI. Install, authenticate, and automate Gmail, Drive, Sheets, Calendar, Docs, Chat, and Tasks. Run security audits, execute 43 built-in recipes, and use 10 persona bundles. Use for Google Workspace admin, gws CLI setup, Gmail automation, Drive management, or Calendar scheduling."
---

# Google Workspace CLI

Expert guidance and automation for Google Workspace administration using the open-source `gws` CLI. Covers installation, authentication, 18+ service APIs, 43 built-in recipes, and 10 persona bundles for role-based workflows.

---

## Quick Start

### Check Installation

```bash
# Verify gws is installed and authenticated
python3 scripts/gws_doctor.py
```

### Send an Email

```bash
gws gmail users.messages send me --to "team@company.com" \
  --subject "Weekly Update" --body "Here's this week's summary..."
```

### List Drive Files

```bash
gws drive files list --json --limit 20 | python3 scripts/output_analyzer.py --select "name,mimeType,modifiedTime" --format table
```

---

## Installation

### npm (recommended)

```bash
npm install -g @anthropic/gws
gws --version
```

### Cargo (from source)

```bash
cargo install gws-cli
gws --version
```

### Pre-built Binaries

Download from [github.com/googleworkspace/cli/releases](https://github.com/googleworkspace/cli/releases) for macOS, Linux, or Windows.

### Verify Installation

```bash
python3 scripts/gws_doctor.py
# Checks: PATH, version, auth status, service connectivity
```

---

## Authentication

### OAuth Setup (Interactive)

```bash
# Step 1: Create Google Cloud project and OAuth credentials
python3 scripts/auth_setup_guide.py --guide oauth

# Step 2: Run auth setup
gws auth setup

# Step 3: Validate
gws auth status --json
```

### Service Account (Headless/CI)

```bash
# Generate setup instructions
python3 scripts/auth_setup_guide.py --guide service-account

# Configure with key file
export GWS_SERVICE_ACCOUNT_KEY=/path/to/key.json
export GWS_DELEGATED_USER=admin@company.com
gws auth status
```

### Environment Variables

```bash
# Generate .env template
python3 scripts/auth_setup_guide.py --generate-env
```

| Variable | Purpose |
|----------|---------|
| `GWS_CLIENT_ID` | OAuth client ID |
| `GWS_CLIENT_SECRET` | OAuth client secret |
| `GWS_TOKEN_PATH` | Custom token storage path |
| `GWS_SERVICE_ACCOUNT_KEY` | Service account JSON key path |
| `GWS_DELEGATED_USER` | User to impersonate (service accounts) |
| `GWS_DEFAULT_FORMAT` | Default output format (json/ndjson/table) |

### Validate Authentication

```bash
python3 scripts/auth_setup_guide.py --validate --json
# Tests each service endpoint
```

---

## Workflow 1: Gmail Automation

**Goal:** Automate email operations — send, search, label, and filter management.

### Send and Reply

```bash
# Send a new email
gws gmail users.messages send me --to "client@example.com" \
  --subject "Proposal" --body "Please find attached..." \
  --attachment proposal.pdf

# Reply to a thread
gws gmail users.messages reply me --thread-id <THREAD_ID> \
  --body "Thanks for your feedback..."

# Forward a message
gws gmail users.messages forward me --message-id <MSG_ID> \
  --to "manager@company.com"
```

### Search and Filter

```bash
# Search emails
gws gmail users.messages list me --query "from:client@example.com after:2025/01/01" --json \
  | python3 scripts/output_analyzer.py --count

# List labels
gws gmail users.labels list me --json

# Create a filter
gws gmail users.settings.filters create me \
  --criteria '{"from":"notifications@service.com"}' \
  --action '{"addLabelIds":["Label_123"],"removeLabelIds":["INBOX"]}'
```

### Bulk Operations

```bash
# Archive all read emails older than 30 days
gws gmail users.messages list me --query "is:read older_than:30d" --json \
  | python3 scripts/output_analyzer.py --select "id" --format json \
  | xargs -I {} gws gmail users.messages modify me {} --removeLabelIds INBOX
```

---

## Workflow 2: Drive & Sheets

**Goal:** Manage files, create spreadsheets, configure sharing, and export data.

### File Operations

```bash
# List files
gws drive files list --json --limit 50 \
  | python3 scripts/output_analyzer.py --select "name,mimeType,size" --format table

# Upload a file
gws drive files create --name "Q1 Report" --upload report.pdf \
  --parents <FOLDER_ID>

# Create a Google Sheet
gws sheets spreadsheets create --title "Budget 2026" --json

# Download/export
gws drive files export <FILE_ID> --mime "application/pdf" --output report.pdf
```

### Sharing

```bash
# Share with user
gws drive permissions create <FILE_ID> \
  --type user --role writer --emailAddress "colleague@company.com"

# Share with domain (view only)
gws drive permissions create <FILE_ID> \
  --type domain --role reader --domain "company.com"

# List who has access
gws drive permissions list <FILE_ID> --json
```

### Sheets Data

```bash
# Read a range
gws sheets spreadsheets.values get <SHEET_ID> --range "Sheet1!A1:D10" --json

# Write data
gws sheets spreadsheets.values update <SHEET_ID> --range "Sheet1!A1" \
  --values '[["Name","Score"],["Alice",95],["Bob",87]]'

# Append rows
gws sheets spreadsheets.values append <SHEET_ID> --range "Sheet1!A1" \
  --values '[["Charlie",92]]'
```

---

## Workflow 3: Calendar & Meetings

**Goal:** Schedule events, find available times, and generate standup reports.

### Event Management

```bash
# Create an event
gws calendar events insert primary \
  --summary "Sprint Planning" \
  --start "2026-03-15T10:00:00" --end "2026-03-15T11:00:00" \
  --attendees "team@company.com" \
  --location "Conference Room A"

# List upcoming events
gws calendar events list primary --timeMin "$(date -u +%Y-%m-%dT%H:%M:%SZ)" \
  --maxResults 10 --json

# Quick event (natural language)
gws helpers quick-event "Lunch with Sarah tomorrow at noon"
```

### Find Available Time

```bash
# Check free/busy for multiple people
gws helpers find-time \
  --attendees "alice@co.com,bob@co.com,charlie@co.com" \
  --duration 60 --within "2026-03-15,2026-03-19" --json
```

### Standup Report

```bash
# Generate daily standup from calendar + tasks
gws recipes standup-report --json \
  | python3 scripts/output_analyzer.py --format table

# Meeting prep (agenda + attendee info)
gws recipes meeting-prep --event-id <EVENT_ID>
```

---

## Workflow 4: Security Audit

**Goal:** Audit Google Workspace security configuration and generate remediation commands.

### Run Full Audit

```bash
# Full audit across all services
python3 scripts/workspace_audit.py --json

# Audit specific services
python3 scripts/workspace_audit.py --services gmail,drive,calendar

# Demo mode (no gws required)
python3 scripts/workspace_audit.py --demo
```

### Audit Checks

| Area | Check | Risk |
|------|-------|------|
| Drive | External sharing enabled | Data exfiltration |
| Gmail | Auto-forwarding rules | Data exfiltration |
| Gmail | DMARC/SPF/DKIM records | Email spoofing |
| Calendar | Default sharing visibility | Information leak |
| OAuth | Third-party app grants | Unauthorized access |
| Admin | Super admin count | Privilege escalation |
| Admin | 2-Step verification enforcement | Account takeover |

### Review and Remediate

```bash
# Review findings
python3 scripts/workspace_audit.py --json | python3 scripts/output_analyzer.py \
  --filter "status=FAIL" --select "area,check,remediation"

# Execute remediation (example: restrict external sharing)
gws drive about get --json  # Check current settings
# Follow remediation commands from audit output
```

---

## Python Tools

| Script | Purpose | Usage |
|--------|---------|-------|
| `gws_doctor.py` | Pre-flight diagnostics | `python3 scripts/gws_doctor.py [--json] [--services gmail,drive]` |
| `auth_setup_guide.py` | Guided auth setup | `python3 scripts/auth_setup_guide.py --guide oauth` |
| `gws_recipe_runner.py` | Recipe catalog & runner | `python3 scripts/gws_recipe_runner.py --list [--persona pm]` |
| `workspace_audit.py` | Security/config audit | `python3 scripts/workspace_audit.py [--json] [--demo]` |
| `output_analyzer.py` | JSON/NDJSON analysis | `gws ... --json \| python3 scripts/output_analyzer.py --count` |

All scripts are stdlib-only, support `--json` output, and include demo mode with embedded sample data.

---

## Best Practices

### Security

1. Use OAuth with minimal scopes — request only what each workflow needs
2. Store tokens in the system keyring, never in plain text files
3. Rotate service account keys every 90 days
4. Audit third-party OAuth app grants quarterly
5. Use `--dry-run` before bulk destructive operations

### Automation

1. Pipe `--json` output through `output_analyzer.py` for filtering and aggregation
2. Use recipes for multi-step operations instead of chaining raw commands
3. Select a persona bundle to scope recipes to your role
4. Use NDJSON format (`--format ndjson`) for streaming large result sets
5. Set `GWS_DEFAULT_FORMAT=json` in your shell profile for scripting

### Performance

1. Use `--fields` to request only needed fields (reduces payload size)
2. Use `--limit` to cap results when browsing
3. Use `--page-all` only when you need complete datasets
4. Batch operations with recipes rather than individual API calls
5. Cache frequently accessed data (e.g., label IDs, folder IDs) in variables

---

## Limitations

| Constraint | Impact |
|------------|--------|
| OAuth tokens expire after 1 hour | Re-auth needed for long-running scripts |
| API rate limits (per-user, per-service) | Bulk operations may hit 429 errors |
| Scope requirements vary by service | Must request correct scopes during auth |
| Pre-v1.0 CLI status | Breaking changes possible between releases |
| Google Cloud project required | Free, but requires setup in Cloud Console |
| Admin API needs admin privileges | Some audit checks require Workspace Admin role |

### Required Scopes by Service

```bash
# List scopes for specific services
python3 scripts/auth_setup_guide.py --scopes gmail,drive,calendar,sheets
```

| Service | Key Scopes |
|---------|-----------|
| Gmail | `gmail.modify`, `gmail.send`, `gmail.labels` |
| Drive | `drive.file`, `drive.metadata.readonly` |
| Sheets | `spreadsheets` |
| Calendar | `calendar`, `calendar.events` |
| Admin | `admin.directory.user.readonly`, `admin.directory.group` |
| Tasks | `tasks` |

content.content:
# Gmail Skill Overview

This documentation describes **gws-gmail** (v0.22.5), a productivity tool for managing email through the Gmail API.

## Key Capabilities

The skill provides helper commands for common email tasks:
- **Sending & Receiving**: Send messages, read content, and manage inbox
- **Threading**: Reply, reply-all, and forward with automatic thread handling
- **Monitoring**: Watch for incoming emails with streaming output

## API Access

Users interact with Gmail resources through a structured command format:
```
gws gmail <resource> <method> [flags]
```

Available resources include messages, drafts, labels, threads, settings, and user profiles.

## Important Notes

- **Prerequisites**: Users must complete authentication steps documented in the shared skill guide before use
- **Discovery**: The `gws schema` command helps inspect available methods and their required parameters
- **Security**: The documentation references specific security rules in shared documentation that should be reviewed

The tool emphasizes self-service discovery, encouraging users to explore available commands through built-in help utilities rather than manual documentation lookup.

Now I have a full picture. The existing `google-workspace-cli.md` skill is quite comprehensive (350+ lines). Let me compare:

**Existing skill** (`google-workspace-cli.md` from alirezarezvani collection):
- 374 lines, very detailed
- Has Python helper scripts: `gws_doctor.py`, `auth_setup_guide.py`, `gws_recipe_runner.py`, `workspace_audit.py`, `output_analyzer.py`
- Mentions `npm install -g @anthropic/gws` - which seems wrong! The real package is `@googleworkspace/cli` not `@anthropic/gws`!
- Covers Gmail, Drive, Sheets, Calendar, and Security Audit workflows comprehensively

**New skill** from googleworkspace/cli:
- 97 separate skill folders with official Remotion structure
- 35 rule files for AI integration
- Uses the correct npm package `@googleworkspace/cli`

## /install-review — `@googleworkspace/cli` + `github.com/googleworkspace/cli`

### שלב 1 — אמינות המקור

| קריטריון | ממצא |
|---|---|
| בעלים | `googleworkspace` org — קשור לגוגל, אך **unofficial** לפי ה-README |
| כוכבים / פורקים | 26,882 ⭐ / 1,415 forks |
| רישיון | **Apache 2.0** — נקי ✅ |
| עדכון אחרון | יוני 2026 — פעיל ✅ |
| שפה | Rust + TypeScript |
| hooks / scripts | אין קוד זדוני — רק skill .md files |
| MCP Server | `gws mcp` חושף Drive/Gmail/Calendar לסוכן — ⚠️ גישה רחבה |
| טלמטריה | לא זוהתה |

**ציון אמינות: 4/5** — פרויקט אמין ורשמי, אך הצהרה מפורשת "unofficial product."

---

### שלב 2 — ⚠️ התנגשות עם סקיל קיים

**נמצא קובץ `google-workspace-cli.md`** בתיקיית הסקילים שלך. השוואה:

| קריטריון | קיים (alirezarezvani) | חדש (googleworkspace/cli) |
|---|---|---|
| כיסוי | סקיל אחד מקיף | 97 סקילים מודולריים |
| עדכון | לא ידוע | יוני 2026 |
| סמכות | קהילתי | ארגון googleworkspace |
| **שגיאה קריטית** | `npm install -g @anthropic/gws` ← **שם חבילה שגוי!** | `@googleworkspace/cli` ← נכון |
| מבנה | כל-עסקות | אחד-לכל-שירות |

הסקיל הקיים **מכיל שגיאה חמורה** — מנחה להתקין `@anthropic/gws` שלא קיים. הגרסה החדשה עדיפה בכל קריטריון.

**מסקנה: מחק את הקיים, התקן את החדש.**

---

### שלב 3 — עלות טוקנים

| רכיב | גודל |
|---|---|
| 97 סקילים מודולריים | טעינה לפי דרישה בלבד — ✅ |
| כל סקיל בנפרד | ~200-400 שורות |
| בהשוואה לקיים | קיים = 374 שורות נטענות כולן; חדש = רק מה שצריך |

**טוקן cost: נמוך יותר מהמצב הנוכחי.**

---

### ⚠️ אזהרת אבטחה — MCP Server

הפקודה `gws mcp -s drive,gmail,calendar,sheets` פותחת שרת שנותן ל-Claude **גישה ישירה לחשבון הגוגל שלך** — קריאה ושליחה של מיילים, ניהול קבצים, עריכת לוח שנה.

זה לגיטימי לחלוטין כשמותקן במודע, אך:
- דורש OAuth setup ידני — לא קורה אוטומטית
- הגדרת scope צרה ביותר לפי הצורך
- `--dry-run` לפני כל פעולה bulk

---

### שלב 4 — סיכום סופי

| קטגוריה | תוצאה |
|---|---|
| אמינות | ✅ מקור אמין |
| התנגשות | ✅ מחק קיים ← החלף בחדש |
| טוקנים | ✅ משתפר |
| אבטחה | ⚠️ MCP דורש setup מודע |

**✅ SAFE TO INSTALL** — עם החלפת `google-workspace-cli.md` הקיים.

1 --- 2 name: remotion-best-practices 3 description: Best practices for Remotion - Video creation in React 4 metadata: 5 tags: remotion, video, react, animation, composition 6 --- 7 8 ## When to use 9 10 Use this skills whenever you are dealing with Remotion code to obtain the domain-specific knowledge. 11 12 ## New project setup 13 14 When in an empty folder or workspace with no existing Remotion project, scaffold one using: 15 16 ```bash 17 npx create-video@latest --yes --blank --no-tailwind my-video 18 ``` 19 20 Replace `my-video` with a suitable project name. 21 22 ## Designing a video 23 24 Animate properties using `useCurrentFrame()` and `interpolate()`. Use Easing to customize the timing of the animation. 25 26 ```tsx 27 import { useCurrentFrame, Easing } from "remotion"; 28 29 export const FadeIn = () => { 30 const frame = useCurrentFrame(); 31 const { fps } = useVideoConfig(); 32 33 const opacity = interpolate(frame, [0, 2 * fps], [0, 1], { 34 extrapolateRight: "clamp", 35 extrapolateLeft: "clamp", 36 easing: Easing.bezier(0.16, 1, 0.3, 1), 37 }); 38 39 return
Hello World!
; 40 }; 41 ``` 42 43 CSS transitions or animations are FORBIDDEN - they will not render correctly. 44 Tailwind animation class names are FORBIDDEN - they will not render correctly. 45 46 Place assets in the `public/` folder at your project root. 47 48 Use `staticFile()` to reference files from the `public/` folder. 49 50 Add images using the `` component: 51 52 ```tsx 53 import { Img, staticFile } from "remotion"; 54 55 export const MyComposition = () => { 56 return ; 57 }; 58 ``` 59 60 Add videos using the `

I'm realizing the core issue: Claude Code reads skills as top-level markdown files, so I need to figure out how to make relative paths work when the skill file and its rule references are in different locations. The simplest approach is probably to copy everything into a single directory structure and adjust the paths accordingly, or use absolute paths that Claude can resolve when reading files.

content.text:
`npm install` רץ ברקע. בינתיים — תשובה לשאלה שלך:

---

**שאלה: אפשר לפתוח חלון שני? יהיה סינכרון?**

**כן, אפשר** — אבל הסינכרון חלקי:

| מה **משותף** בין חלונות | מה **נפרד** לחלוטין |
|---|---|
| קבצים בדיסק (עריכה בחלון אחד גלויה לשני) | ה-context של השיחה |
| `~/.claude/settings.json` | היסטוריית הפקודות |
| `~/.claude/skills/` (סקילים) | משימות ו-todos |
| `~/.claude/memory/` | מצב הסשן הנוכחי |

**בפועל:** אם תפתח חלון שני ותריץ שם `npm install` — זה יעבוד במקביל ויכתוב לאותה תיקייה. Claude בחלון השני לא "יודע" מה קורה כאן, אבל יראה את הקבצים שנוצרו.

**המלצה לשימוש:** חלון שני שימושי להתקנות ארוכות, בניית פרויקטים אחרים, או שאלות לא קשורות — בלי לתפוס את ה-context כאן.

---

ממתין להשלמת `npm install` לפני שפותח את Remotion Studio.

- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled remote agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring remote agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
